coredata.dc:title: On the formation of hydrophobic carbon quantum dots Langmuir films and their transfer onto solid substrates
coredata.dc:creator: Bodik, Michal
Siffalovic, Peter
Nadazdy, Peter
Benkovicova, Monika
Markovic, Zoran
Chlpik, Juraj
Cirak, Julius
Kotlar, Mario
Micusik, Matej
Jergel, Matej
Majkova, Eva
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we present a study of the Langmuir film formation composed of hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (hCQDs). We studied the kinetics of hCQDs pyrolysis and optimized the reaction time for maximum photoluminescence. The resulting hCQDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoemission spectroscopy and grazing-incidence X-ray diffraction. The process of monolayer formation was studied by monitoring surface pressure, surface potential, elastic modulus and Brewster angle microscopy in real time under continuous compression conditions. The monolayer transferred onto a silicon wafer was examined on the nano- and micro-scales by means of atomic force microscopy and confocal fluorescence microscopy, respectively.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of the transferred hCQDs monolayer

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: University Science Park of STU Bratislava

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge support of the APVV-0308-11, APVV-14-0745, APVV-15-0641, APVV-14-0120, VEGA 2/0092/18 projects. The work was done during implementation of the projects Applied Research of Advanced Photovoltaic Cells, ITMS code 26240220047 (50%), CENTE II, ITMS code 26240120019 (25%), and University Science Park of STU Bratislava, ITMS code 26240220084, supported by the Research & Development Operational Program funded by the ERDF. This research was supported by the SASPRO Programme project 1237/02/02-b . The research leading to these results has received funding from the People Programme (Marie Curie Actions) European Union's Seventh Framework Programme under REA grant agreement No. 609427 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorbance and (b) photoluminescence spectra of hCQDs samples taken at different times during reaction.

The integral PL intensity of hCQDs corrected for self-absorption as a function of the reaction time. Black dots represent measured data and the red curve Boltzmann fit. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) TEM micrographs of hCQDs. The insert shows a single highly crystalline hCQD nanoparticle. The distance between lattice fringes corresponds to (100) lattice spacing of graphite, (b) the X-ray diffraction profile extracted from GIWAXS pattern.

The evolution of (a) surface pressure, elastic modulus, (b) monolayer coverage and (c) surface potential during a continuous film compression. The red dashed line indicates the point of monolayer collapse. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

PSD curves of five hCQDs sub-monolayer films having increasing surface pressure. The intercept of two orange lines defines a characteristic spatial cutoff frequency k c. The grey arrow indicates the increasing trend of k c with the increasing surface pressure.
The lateral correlation length derived from PSD as a function of the film area.
AFM image of the monolayer hCQD film on a silicon wafer.
The confocal PL map overlaid with a BW optical image on the background.
The optical transmission of hCQD monolayer transferred onto a fused silica substrate.
Apparent surface chemical composition of hCQDs on Si wafer as determined by XPS.

head.ce:title: On the formation of hydrophobic carbon quantum dots Langmuir films and their transfer onto solid substrates

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

Slovak University of Technology in Bratislava

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Slovak University of Technology, University Science Park Bratislava Centre, Vazovova 5, 812 43 Bratislava, Slovakia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we present a study of the Langmuir film formation composed of hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (hCQDs). We studied the kinetics of hCQDs pyrolysis and optimized the reaction time for maximum photoluminescence. The resulting hCQDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoemission spectroscopy and grazing-incidence X-ray diffraction. The process of monolayer formation was studied by monitoring surface pressure, surface potential, elastic modulus and Brewster angle microscopy in real time under continuous compression conditions. The monolayer transferred onto a silicon wafer was examined on the nano- and micro-scales by means of atomic force microscopy and confocal fluorescence microscopy, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials have attracted large amount of interest because of variety of possible applications [1]. Success of fullerenes and carbon nanotubes [2] along with diamond-like carbon [3,4] has initiated intense research of new carbon materials. A new carbon allotrope and the first ever confirmed two-dimensional material — graphene [5] — proved that the research of carbon nanomaterials has still large potential and can lead to breakthrough discoveries.

The carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional allotropes of carbon with the size below 10 nm. CQDs were discovered in 2004 as a byproduct of carbon nanotubes preparation [6]. CQDs quickly became promising nanomaterial in a large number of applications, especially due to many different cost-effective synthesis routes [7,8]. It is in biomedicine, where their favorable small size, biocompatibility and fluorescence properties found the most promising applications [7,9]. Alternatively, CQDs found employment in sensors, catalytically active materials, solar cells, or in LEDs [10–16]. Another appealing application of CQDs is their use as a stand-alone antibacterial coating or filler in advanced composite materials for medical use [17–19]. The antibacterial effect of CQDs is mediated through the formation of singlet oxygen state [20] where CQDs act as a photocatalyst for excitation of oxygen molecules [19].

Basic research of CQDs opens an opportunity to better understand their physicochemical properties and to improve the performance of final products with embedded CQDs. A deeper knowledge of the self-assembly process of CQDs is essential for targeted fabrication of thin continuous films for future applications. This is particularly important for those based on layering the CQDs material [16,21] where tailoring the deposition process without a prior knowledge of the monolayer formation is a difficult task.

A controlled compression of Langmuir films at the air-water interface is a well-established technique for the studies of monolayer formation from hydrophobic nanomaterials [22,23]. The Langmuir technique is commonly used when a continuous and spatially homogeneous layer over a large-scale area is required. The Langmuir-Blodgett (LB) or Langmuir-Schaefer (LS) techniques are routinely employed for the preparation of graphene or graphene oxide monolayers on solid substrates [24–28] as well as for the preparation of nanoparticle layers [29–31]. LB or LS layers are of high quality and are superior to spin-coated or drop-coated layers. The technique has also advantage in the controllability of the whole process. The LB process allows monitoring of the surface pressure or surface potential changes and it is possible to find different phases during the compression. This allows to control the density of the LB film and to craft the film according to the requirements of the application. The LB method was used by Park et al. [32], who prepared a monolayer of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a doping layer for graphene film. During compression, GQDs tend to form monolayer islands at lower surface pressures and further compression causes formation of a continuous monolayer film of GQDs between the surface pressure of 29 mN/m and 32 mN/m. Further compression results in a formation of multilayer. However, a deeper study to determine the critical pressure where the monolayer starts to be formed is missing.

Herein we study the kinetics of Langmuir film formation of hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (hCQDs). First the hydrothermal decomposition reaction used for synthesis of hCQDs is studied in detail. The probes taken at different reaction times were characterized by UV-VIS-NIR absorption spectroscopy and photoluminescence measurements. An optimum duration of reaction with the highest photoluminescence yield of hCQDs was found.

The work is focused on the process of monolayer formation of hCQDs. The formation process was monitored by tracking the surface pressure and surface potential along with in-situ Brewster angle microscopy (BAM) during continuous compression of Langmuir film. The Langmuir-Schaefer films transferred onto a silicon wafer were analyzed by atomic force microscopy (AFM) and confocal fluorescence mapping which complement the data obtained from Langmuir films on water subphase. Additional ex-situ analyses of hCQDs including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), grazing-incidence wide-angle X-ray scattering (GIWAXS), X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS), and contact angle measurements were done to support the previous measurements.

The hCQD solution was prepared by pyrolysis of polyoxyethylene-polyoxypropylene-polyoxyethylene triblock copolymer (Pluronic PF-68) by a bottom-up condensation method similar to the process used by Mitra et al. [33]. Briefly, 1 g of Pluronic PF-68 was dissolved in 100 ml of ultrapure water (Milli-Q, 18 MΩ·cm) using ultrasonic bath. Subsequently, 200 ml of phosphoric acid was added, and the mixture was magnetically stirred on a hot plate at 250 °C. The reaction was terminated by spontaneous cooling the mixture down to ambient temperature. Afterwards, an additional amount of 250 ml of water was added. In the end, 300 ml of chloroform (≥99%, stabilized with 0.5–1% of ethanol) was added, while continuously stirring the solution. Phosphoric acid is insoluble in non-polar solvents. Therefore, after mixing the solution with chloroform we can clearly observe the separation of chloroform and acidic phase. The mixture was then left still to gradually separate the organic phase from the chloroform one. The transfer is done carefully with a glass pipette and part of the chloroform fraction, that is in contact with acid is decanted with acid. The chloroform solution, containing hCQDs was centrifuged at 60,000g for 60 min. In the end, the sediment was removed, and the supernatant was used for further study.

For the studies of hCQDs monolayer formation, we used a KSV-NIMA Langmuir trough (Biolin Scientific OY, Finland). The subphase used in our experiment was ultrapure water (Milli-Q, 18 MΩ·cm). The hCQDs dispersed in chloroform were drop-wisely added onto the water subphase using a glass microsyringe. The surface pressure after dropping of approximately 3 ml of hCQDs increased up to 12 mN/m. The surface pressure and surface potential were continuously recorded by a Wilhelmy plate and vibrating Kelvin probe (Trek INC., USA), respectively. The elastic modulus is proportional to the first derivation of the surface pressure curve [34]. The BAM images were acquired repeatedly after each increase of surface pressure by 0.25 mN/m during the film compression.

For the surface morphology studies using AFM and confocal photoluminescence mapping, the hCQDs monolayer film was transferred onto a silicon wafer with native silicon oxide layer. For the absorption measurements, the hCQDs film was deposited onto a fused silica wafer. Prior to the deposition, the substrates were cleaned by acetone, isopropanol, UV-ozone and submerged into the water subphase. The hCQDs film was deposited at 31 mN/m by a modified Langmuir-Schaefer method described in detail elsewhere [23].

The BAM was performed by a spectroscopic imaging ellipsometer (Accurion GmbH, Germany) with Nikon objective (5×, NA = 0.13). We developed a software code to automatically analyze the acquired BAM images and localize the hCQDs islands using an adaptive threshold analysis. The radially averaged power spectral densities (PSD) of spatial island distribution and the total island coverage were calculated automatically.

The UV–VIS-NIR absorbance was measured by a Solidspec-3700 (SHIMAZDU, Japan) spectrophotometer with a spectral measurement step of 1 nm and a monochromator bandwidth of 2 nm. The measurements were performed in transmission mode while hCQDs were deposited onto a fused silica glass instead the silicon wafer.

The photoluminescence (PL) of hCQDs solutions at various reaction stages was measured using a spectrofluorometer Fluorolog-3 (Horiba, France). The excitation wavelength was set to 355 nm and the excitation bandwidth was 1 nm. The PL emission measurement bandwidth and the step size were equally set to 1 nm.

The as-prepared hCQDs solutions in chloroform were dropped and dried on standard TEM grids with ultrathin amorphous carbon films and inspected by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, ARM200CF, Jeol).

The two-dimensional X-ray diffraction pattern of thick hCQDs film drop casted onto Si wafer was measured by means of GIWAXS technique using a custom-designed Nanostar system (Bruker, USA) equipped with a liquid metal-jet anode X-ray generator (Excillum, Sweden).

XPS signals of hCQDs film drop casted onto Si wafer were recorded using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK) equipped with a micro-focused, monochromatic Al Ka X-ray source (1486.6 eV). An X-ray beam of 400 mm size was used at 6 mA × 12 kV. The spectra were acquired in the constant analyzer energy mode with a pass energy of 50 eV. Charge compensation was achieved with the system flood gun. The Thermo Scientific Avantage software, version 5.981 (Thermo Fisher Scientific), was used for digital acquisition and data processing.
The transferred Langmuir film of hCQDs on Si wafer was analyzed with a MultiMode 8 AFM device (Bruker, Germany) equipped with ScanAsyst-Air AFM tips.

The spatially-resolved PL maps were measured on a confocal Raman microscope (CRM) Alpha300R+ (WiTec, Germany), with an excitation wavelength of 355 nm and an effective confocal pinhole with a diameter of 50 μm. The excitation laser was focused on the sample using a 50× objective (NA = 0.8) and the PL spectra were collected by an optical fiber and recorded by a spectrophotometer with attached CCD camera.
The advancing and receding contact angles were measured by a Cam200 instrument (KSV instruments LTD, Finland) with ultrapure water used as a probing liquid.

To optimize the hCQDs PL yield, we studied the temporal evolution of synthesis reaction [35]. The pyrolysis process was sampled by photoabsorption and PL measurements, taking out successively 5 ml volumes. We analyzed the reaction starting from 83 min up to 370 min after we started the heating of the initial mixture. The eight differently colored sample solutions of hCQD in phosphoric acid/water mixture were analyzed. For the measurements of highly absorbing samples the solution was diluted with demineralized water to the concentration with minimal visible absorption. The resulting spectra of diluted samples were multiplied by corresponding dilution factor prior evaluation. The photoabsorption and PL measurements are shown in Fig. 1a and b, respectively. The absorbance of the sample after 370 min of reaction is halved for better readability in Fig. 1.

The two distinct absorption peaks superimposed on a broad absorption background typical for carbon nanomaterials including graphene [8] can be observed in Fig. 1a. The absorption peaks located at 242 nm and 360 nm can be associated with π–π* transitions of CC [36–39] and δ–π* transitions of CO bonds [40,41], respectively. With increasing the reaction time, the absolute absorbance rises without any significant change in the spectrum profile. In order to quantitatively compare the PL evolution at different reaction stages, we have to eliminate the effect of re-absorption on PL spectra. From the Beer-Lambert law it follows, that we have to multiply the measured PL with an exponential of the measured absorbance in the corresponding part of spectra in order to calculate the intrinsic photoluminescence intensity. The PL maximum shown in Fig. 1b is observed at approximately 425 nm. In some cases, a weak peak from Raman scattering from water is visible in the spectrum at 405 nm. This is caused by the dilution of the sample with water.

To easily follow the PL changes in the course of reaction, we plotted the integral intensities of PL peaks after the correction for self-absorption effect as a function of the reaction time in Fig. 2 . The temporal evolution of PL integral intensity is well described by Boltzmann growth model shown as red line in Fig. 2.

The nucleation and growth of the nanoparticles carbonaceous core can be readily associated with the observed increase of the integral PL intensity at the early reaction times [42]. The saturation of the integral PL intensity after 3 h suggests termination of the nanoparticles growth process, probably due to a competing agglomeration. In all experiments with the hCQDs Langmuir films, nanoparticle fractions produced by the pyrolysis interrupted after 2 h were used. This guarantees the hCQDs with high PL yield while maintaining low absorption.
To analyze the size distribution and shape of hCQDs, we performed TEM. Fig. 3a shows a TEM micrograph of multiple hCQDs.

The hCQDs particles have nearly spherical shape and an average diameter of 5 nm with a size dispersion of 19%. The radially integrated intensity from GIWAXS pattern is shown in Fig. 3b. A broad 002 diffraction peak is located at q = 1.63 Å−1 which corresponds to a −d spacing of 3.84 Å or a unit lattice c parameter of 7.68 Å (space group P63/mmc). This is much larger than the undistorted c lattice parameter of graphite of 6.7 Å, suggesting a rather imperfect graphitization during pyrolysis process. This is similar to other published data [43].

XPS analysis of high resolution spectra of the elements detected on the surface (C, O, Cl and Si) is summarized in Table 1 . Si 2p signal comes from the Si substrate indicating very thin hCQDs film. Strong C 1s signal at ~284.3 eV represents the sp2 carbon of hCQD [Avantage software 5.981, XPS knowledge database (UK: ThermoFisher Scientific)] and thus confirms the successful synthesis of hCQD. Several oxygen groups are present on the surface (CO, CO, OCO) indicating some degree of oxidation. Besides oxygen also some small amount of chlorine (0.5 at.%) is present. It comes probably from chloroform reaction with hCQD and formation of a covalent bond (signal at ~200 eV corresponding to CCl) or chloroform itself was somehow trapped in the final product.

The formation of hCQDs Langmuir films was studied in a conventional Langmuir-Blodgett trough by monitoring the surface pressure, surface potential and BAM during continuous compression of the film area. The experiment itself was performed in two separate runs. It the first run, we recorded only the BAM images along with the surface pressure measurements. In the second run, we performed the surface potential measurements. Fig. 4 shows the evolution of surface pressure, elastic modulus, surface potential and monolayer coverage calculated from BAM images.

The film area below 120 cm2 was not accessible for BAM measurements due to spatial restrictions imposed by the BAM optical setup. On the other hand, the surface potential measurements were conducted down to 50 cm2 which is important for a reliable estimation of the critical pressure Πc corresponding to a monolayer collapse. The hCQDs monolayer formation is completed at a surface pressure of approximately Πc = 28 mN/m. The monolayer coverage of 95% agrees well with the position of maximum elastic modulus. In contrast to the molecular Langmuir monolayers [44], the hCQDs monolayers are mechanically rigid which gives rise to local spatial inhomogeneities and micro- and nanometer-sized voids in films. This can account for minor discrepancies observed between the maximum of elastic modulus and the maximum of monolayer coverage estimated by BAM. Above the critical pressure Πc, the monolayer begins to collapse which is evidenced by a decrease in elastic modulus [34]. In the first order approximation, the linear increase of surface potential during the growth of the first and second layer can be employed to independently validate the critical pressure Πc based on a monitoring of the mechano-elastic properties. The range of surface areas close to the monolayer collapse has to be excluded from the evaluation and the linear asymptotical behavior of surface potential is used to construct a cross-over point determining the critical pressure Πc. The value Πc = 29 ± 4 mN/m agrees well with the value of critical pressure for the monolayer collapse determined from elastic modulus. The modified Langmuir-Schaefer deposition of hCQDs is typically done at a surface pressure of 31 mN/m [45] which guarantees a highly uniform monolayer deposition.

The hCQDs monolayer formation was further studied by analyzing the BAM images in reciprocal space. A real-time tracking of the radially averaged power spectral density (PSD) functions derived from BAM images during the film compression provides information on the characteristic spatial frequencies governing morphology of the closing hCQDs monolayer. Fig. 5 shows selected PSD functions for five sub-monolayers with successively increasing surface pressures.

A common feature of all PSD functions is a constant plateau at low spatial frequencies followed by a monotonously decreasing part at high spatial frequencies which is a typical shape of PSD functions of self-affine surfaces [46]. An intercept of the two extrapolated linear fits of these regions in log-log scale is used to determine a cutoff frequency k c and its reciprocal value, which is the lateral correlation length ξ. The hCQDs islands start to be formed at low surface pressures as visualized by BAM, being homogenously distributed over the subphase area. Their concentration increases with pressure due to the gradually closing trough barriers as well as new islands formation which results in the observed increase of cutoff frequency. The island coalescence would result in the opposite trend; i.e. a decrease of cutoff frequency. However, it cannot be excluded as a simultaneous but less dominant effect, which is overlaid by the previous factors.

The lateral correlation length ξ as a function of the film area is shown in Fig. 6 . It is proportional to the mean distance between the hCQDs islands on the water subphase. Assuming uniformly distributed non-interacting islands at low surface pressures, the lateral correlation length should scale as a square root of the total film area. At high surface pressures with strongly increasing elastic modulus, this simple scaling does not hold any more as the repulsive interaction between the adjacent islands comes into play. At the surface area A c corresponding to the critical pressure Πc for the monolayer collapse, the lateral correlation length is equal to the mean size of hCQDs and thus is well below the resolution limit of BAM technique. A square root fit of the lateral correlation length along with the confidence interval of the fit is shown in Fig. 6. At large film areas, the lateral correlation length of hCQDs islands follows the simple square-root dependence within 95% confidence limit. At small film areas, the experimental data exhibit a clear deviation from this scaling, being much larger due to the significant increase of elastic modulus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the transferred hCQDs monolayer
ce:section.ce:para: The hCQDs monolayer was transferred by the modified Langmuir-Schaefer method as described in the experimental section. The AFM analysis of surface morphology was performed to investigate the film roughness at high spatial frequencies. Fig. 7 shows AFM image of the hCQDs monolayer film on a silicon wafer. The silicon substrate has a mean RMS roughness of approximately 2 Å. After the deposition of hCQDs film, the RMS roughness changed to almost 4 Å. The hCQDs monolayer produces exceptionally smooth surface and almost perfectly replicates the substrate morphology in spite of the spherical shape of individual hCQDs.

To assess homogeneity of the transferred hCQDs monolayer film with sub-micron resolution, we performed a PL mapping using the scanning confocal microscopy. The PL map in Fig. 8 shows a uniform layer of hCQDs. Surface contamination introduced during the sample handling is observable as random dots.

To evaluate hydrophobic nature of hCQDs, the contact angle (CA) analysis is commonly used. However, the static CA provides only partial information about the material wettability. The CA hysteresis, i.e. the difference between advancing and receding CA, offers also a dynamic view of the wettability phenomenon [47–49]. We studied the CA hysteresis of hCQDs films and its long-term stability on three different samples. The results in Table 2 indicate a high stability of CA hysteresis over the period of one month after the deposition and storage under ambient conditions.

The measured large CA hysteresis is in contradiction with the low measured surface roughness. Materials with low roughness typically exhibit low CA hysteresis. The measured CA hysteresis suggests the presence of pinning centers on the sample [48,49]. The pinning centers can be chemical or structural inhomogeneities. In the case of hCQDs monolayer, chemical inhomogeneities are less probable as the entire film is made of the same material. As for structural inhomogeneities, the AFM analysis of hCQDs films revealed nanometer-sized pits (Fig. 7) that are characteristic for Langmuir films prepared by compression of nanoparticles. This suggests that the Cassie-Baxter wetting state [49] is responsible for the observed large CA hysteresis.
The transmittance of hCQD monolayer film deposited on a fused silica substrate is shown in Fig. 9 .

The hCQDs monolayer has low absorption in the visible part of spectrum. The higher extinction in the UV part of spectrum is in agreement with the absorption spectra measured in solutions (Fig. 1a). In addition to the antibacterial properties under light illumination [17,18], the low absorbance of hCQDs renders them suitable for inexpensive transparent hydrophobic coatings.

We prepared the solution of hydrophobic hCQDs in chloroform by pyrolysis of pluronic PF-68 polymer. We investigated the kinetics of reaction by means of absorbance and photoluminescence measurements and found an optimum duration of hCQDs pyrolysis having a maximum photoluminescence yield. We analyzed the hCQDs using the TEM, XPS and GIWAXS techniques, which confirmed the crystalline carbonaceous nanoparticle core and average nanoparticle size of 5 nm, respectively. The real-time monitoring of surface pressure, surface potential, elastic modulus and BAM was employed to study the hCQDs monolayer formation on the water surface. The monolayer formation exhibits scenario typical for nanoparticle systems. First, the individual hCQDs particles form islands on the water surface. The compression of the film surface results in an increase of the surface pressure and islands surface density as verified by BAM. Simultaneously, a substantial increase in elastic modulus as a result of the repulsive forces between individual hCQDs is observed. At a critical surface pressure of 29 mN/m, elastic modulus comes to a maximum and a closed monolayer film is formed. This precedes the monolayer collapse and the second layer formation. The closed hCQDs monolayer film was transferred onto a flat silicon substrate. A uniform monolayer with a RMS roughness of only 4 Å was confirmed by AFM measurements. The optical absorbance of monolayer film is negligible, being almost zero in the visible range of spectrum. The contact angle measurements confirmed hydrophobic nature of the hCQDs monolayer with a considerably large hysteresis between the advancing and receding contact angles. The photoluminescence mapping confirmed good uniformity of hCQDs monolayers on the micrometer scale.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge support of the APVV-0308-11, APVV-14-0745, APVV-15-0641, APVV-14-0120, VEGA 2/0092/18 projects. The work was done during implementation of the projects Applied Research of Advanced Photovoltaic Cells, ITMS code 26240220047 (50%), CENTE II, ITMS code 26240120019 (25%), and University Science Park of STU Bratislava, ITMS code 26240220084, supported by the Research & Development Operational Program funded by the ERDF. This research was supported by the SASPRO Programme project 1237/02/02-b. The research leading to these results has received funding from the People Programme (Marie Curie Actions) European Union's Seventh Framework Programme under REA grant agreement No. 609427.
